steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Electromagnetic induction occurs when…
options: A current is passed through a wire near a magnet
A conductor moves through a magnetic field (or the field through a conductor)
Two magnets are placed close together
A magnet is held still next to a coil of wire
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Electromagnetic induction requires relative motion between a conductor and a magnetic field — or a changing magnetic field. A stationary magnet next to a coil produces no induction and therefore no current.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Induction happens when there is relative motion between a conductor and a magnetic field. Without motion or a changing field, there is no induced EMF or current.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.7.4 — Electromagnetic induction

steps.title: Generators and Electromagnetic Induction
steps.bodyHTML: Electromagnetic induction is the process by which a potential difference (EMF) is induced in a conductor when there is relative motion between the conductor and a magnetic field. A generator uses this principle to convert kinetic energy into electrical energy by rotating a coil within a magnetic field. The induced EMF can be increased by moving the conductor faster, using a stronger magnet, or increasing the number of turns on the coil.

relationships: More coil turns → greater induced EMF

commonErrors: Thinking a stationary magnet near a coil induces a current
Confusing generators (motion → electricity) with motors (electricity → motion)
Forgetting that reversing the direction of motion reverses the induced current

aqa.questionText: Describe three ways to increase the size of the induced EMF in an electromagnetic generator.

steps.label: Factor 1 — speed of rotation
steps.content: Rotate the coil faster (increase the speed of relative motion between the coil and the magnetic field).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — increase speed / rate of change of flux

steps.label: Factor 2 — magnetic field strength
steps.content: Use a stronger magnet to increase the magnetic flux density through the coil.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — stronger magnet / greater field strength

steps.label: Factor 3 — number of turns
steps.content: Increase the number of turns on the coil so that more conductors cut through the field lines each cycle.

aqa.finalAnswer: Rotate faster; use a stronger magnet; increase the number of coil turns.

aqa.questionText: A magnet is held stationary inside a coil of wire. What is the induced current?
markSchemeSteps: No relative motion between magnet and coil means no change in flux — so no induced EMF and no current ✓
aqa.commonMistake: A common error is to think a magnet always causes a current — motion (or a changing field) is essential for induction.
